R ‘i v1 -IJi•\
PAIR P L ITI(\
PRACTICES COM MISS ION
February 28,
1996
Lori Howard
City of Santee
10601 Magnolia Avenue
Santee,
California
92071-1266
Re:
Your Request for
Informal Assistance
Our File No.
1-96-035
Dear Ms.
Howard:
This is in reference to your request for advice regarding
your responsibilities as
a public official under the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the “Act”).
1
Since you have not provided any specific government decision to be
considered,
we are treating your request as
one for informal
assistance. 2
FACTS
You stated in your request that you were elected to the
Santee City Council in November 1994,
and you have owned a coffee
shop in a redevelopment area of the city since August 1992.
The
coffee shop is in a complex anchored by a movie theater.
In
August 1998,
you will need to move your business.
There is a site
nearby that the city owns and plans to sell to developers who are
planning to build a movie theatre on the site.
You would like to
relocate your business to the new area of development.
You
mentioned in our telephone conversation on January 31,
1996, that
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections
18000—18995.
2
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3).)
428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-5660


rLL
INL).
Page
2
you sit on the Town Center Committee,
which is involved with the
redevelopment area
-
In a letter you faxed me on February 9,
1996,
in response to
my letter requesting additional facts,
you explained that you are
moving your business in
1998 because your long—term lease was not
extended past August 1998.
The anchor of your business’
current
complex is the MIC theatre,
which plans to move across the street
to the new Town Center location.
You hope to move with the
AMC
Theatre because your business depends on foot traffic from the
theatre and you have a good working relationship with the theatre.
The city owns the property on which the new Town Center will
be built and the Town Center Committee consists of two
councilmembers,
one public member and city staff employees.
The
committee presents recommendations to the city council.
The
recommendations are often hotly debated by the council and are not
always accepted.
The negotiations with the developers regarding the leasing of
the Town Center are being handled by a consultant employed by the
city.
Neither the Town Center Committee nor the city council are
involved with the negotiations.
To your knowledge,
there have
been no official agreements made regarding the theatre or any
other vendor.
CONCLUSION
You may not participate in any decision that will have
a
material financial effect on your business entity.
If you
determine that the decisions regarding the planned entertainment
complex in the Town Center will materiaLly financially affect your
business,
you must disqualify yourself from that decision.
ANALYSIS
Economic Interests
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official from
making, participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest.
You are a public official
by virtue of your seat on the City of Santee City Council.
(Section 82048.)
Section 87103 specifies that a public official has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally,
on the
official or a member of his or her immediate family or on:
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth one
thousand dollars
($l,0b0)
or more.


Page
3
(b)
Any real property in which the public official
has
a direct or indirect interest worth one thousand
dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(c)
Any source of income,
other than gifts
and
other than loans
by a commercial lending institution in
the regular course of business on terms available to the
public without regard to official status,
aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars
($250)
or more in value
provided to,
received by or promised to the public
official within
12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
(Section 87103.)
You have stated that you have a coffee shop business located
in the redevelopment area of the City of Santee.
You have a
business entity interest pursuant to Section 87103(b).
In
addition,
you have a real property interest in the form of a long-
term leasehold.
(Section 82033; Regulation 18233.)
Thus,
you may
not partidipate in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that
the decision will materially financially affect your economic
interests.
In addition to your seat on the city council,
you sit on the
Town Center Committee.
The Town Center Committee is an advisory
committee appointed by the city council to advise the council
regarding the redevelopment of the Town Center.
(Woodward Advice
Letter,
No.
A—95-049.)
As noted,
Section 87100 requires
a public official to refrain
from using his or her official position to influence a
governmental decision in which the official has a financial
interest.
Regulation l8700.l(a)•provides that with regard to a
governmental decision which is within or before the official’s
own
agency,
or any agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary
control of the official’s agency, the official is attempting to
use his or her official position to influence the decision if,
for
the purpose of influencing the decision, the official contacts,
or
appears before,
or otherwise attempts to influence,
any member,
officer,
employee or consultant of the agency.
(Regulations
18700
and 18700.1.)
Since you are a public official on the city
council, this provision would apply to influencing the Town Center
Committee or any advisory bodies that are appointed by the city
council.
(Jones Advice Letter,
1-92-261.)
Therefore, if you determine that a recommendation made by the
Town Center Committee will have a material financial effect on
your economic interests, then you must disqualify yourself from
the decision to avoid influencing the Town Center Committee or the
city council.


Page4
Foreseeability and Materiality
You must disqualify yourself from any decision that will
have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your
economic interests.
An effect is considered reasonably
foreseeable if there is
a substantial likelihood that it will
occur.
(In re Thorner
(1975)
1
FPPC Ops.
198.)
The Commission has adopted a series of regulations which
provide guidance concerning whether the foreseeable financial
effects of a decision are material.
These regulations apply
different standards depending on whether the decision will
directly or indirectly affect the officiaWs economic interest.
3
Business Entity
The effect of a decision on a business entity is material if
the business entity is directly involved in the decision.
The
business entity is directly involved if the person or business
entit7:
(1)
Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will
be made by filing an application, claim,
appeal,
or
similar request or;
(2)
Is a named party in,
or is the subject of,
the
proceeding concerning the decision before the official
or the official’s agency.
(3)
A person or business entity is the subject of
a
proceeding if a decision involves the issuance,
renewal,
approval,
denial or revocation of any license, permit,
or other entitlement to,
or contract with, the subject
person or business entity.
(Regulation 18702.1(b).)
If the business entity is not directly involved,
you must
look at the materiality regulations for decisions where the
Regulation 18703 provides an exception where the material
financial effect on the official’s interest is not distinguishable
from the effect on the public generally.
(Section 87103;
Regulation 18703.)
Since your business entity is within the Town
Center, it does not appear that the test for the exception will be
met.
However,
we have enclosed the regulation for your
consideration.
If you do believe the “public generally” exception
will apply or if you have any further questions, please feel free
to contact us at
(916)
322-5660.


Page
5
official’s interests are not directly involved.
For
a relatively
small business please refer to Regulation 18702.2(g)
which
provides that the effect is material if:
(1)
The decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year of
$10,000 or more;
or
(2)
The decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal
year in the amount of $2,500 or more;
or
(3)
The decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the value of assets or liabilities of
$10,000 or more.
We do not have enough facts to indicate how your business
entity will be affected by the decisions regarding the Town
Center.
You must make this factual determination on your
own by
referring to the above regulations with respect to each decision.
Even if you determine that a decision has
a material financial
effect on your business entity,
the Act’s conflict—of—interest
provisions do not require you to resign from the city council,
but you must disqualify yourself from the decision.
(Sullivan
Advice Letter,
No.
A-94-175.)
Real Property Interest
The effect on your leasehold interest will be material if any
of the following applies:
(a)
The decision will change the legallyallowable
use of the leased property,
and the lessee has a right
to sublease the property;
(b)
It is reasonably foreseeable that the lessee
will change the actual use of the property as a result
of the decision;
(c)
It is reasonably foreseeable that the decision
will result in a change in the actual use of property
within 300 feet of the leased property,
and the changed
use will significantly enhance or significantly decrease
the use or enjoyment of the leased property;
(d)
The decision will increase or decrease the
amount of rent for the leased property by $250 or
5
percent, whichever is greater, during any 12—month
period following the decision; or


Page
6
(e)
The decision will result in a change in the
termination date of the lease.
(Regulation 18702.4.)
As with your business entity,
you have not provided enough
information to determine whether your leasehold interest will be
materially affected by the decisions of the Town Center Committee.
Currently,
the only leasehold interest you have is in the property
where your business is currently located.
If you move to the Town
Center and sign a lease at that location, it will be more likely
that your new leasehold interest,
and your business entity
interest, will be materially affected by the Town Center
Committee.
If you have any further questions,
please feel free to
contact me at
(916)
322—5660.
Sincerely,
Steven
G.
Churchwell
General Counsel
/
By:
Liane Rahdolph
-
Counsel,
Legal Division
SGC:LR: ak

material financial effect on your business entity.

determine that the decisions regarding the planned entertainment
complex in the Town Center will materiaLly financially affect your
business,
you must disqualify yourself from that decision.
